era_context: The Akkadian Empire is in its final phase of decline under the successors of Naram-Sin. Egypt's Sixth Dynasty continues its fragmentation. The Indus Valley Civilization remains in its mature urban phase. This is a transitional year within broader processes of political collapse and reorganization across the Old World.

events.title: Akkadian Empire: Continued fragmentation under post-Naram-Sin rulers
events.description: Following Naram-Sin's death (c. 2200 BCE), his successors struggle to maintain Akkadian hegemony. Shar-kali-sharri (r. c. 2217–2193 BCE) rules a progressively weakened state as Gutian pressure mounts and regional city-states reassert autonomy. Administrative records show declining royal resources and reduced military campaigns. The empire's trade networks, once extensive, contract. This year falls within the period of accelerating decentralization that will culminate in the empire's dissolution by c. 2154 BCE.
events.certainty_note: The general trajectory is secure, but precise year-to-year events are not attested. This entry describes the broader context rather than a specific event.

events.title: Egypt: Sixth Dynasty provincial autonomy increases
events.description: Nomarchs (provincial governors) in Upper and Lower Egypt continue to consolidate hereditary power and local authority. Royal inscriptions and tomb biographies from this period show nomarchs conducting independent military campaigns, controlling local resources, and building monuments without explicit royal sanction. The pharaoh's ability to command resources for major building projects (pyramids, temples) continues to decline. This fragmentation is a defining characteristic of the late Sixth Dynasty.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of reduced royal construction

events.certainty_note: The process is well-attested, but specific events in this particular year are not documented.

disconfirming_evidence: No specific disconfirming evidence for events in this year, as no particular events are attested. The broader narrative of Akkadian and Egyptian decline is secure, but the year -2191 itself has no documented events in surviving sources.
historiographic_note: This year is essentially undocumented in surviving sources. The events described are inferred from the broader historical trajectory visible in administrative records, inscriptions, and archaeological stratigraphy spanning decades. No contemporary source specifically records events of -2191 BCE. The historical record for this period is a patchwork of fragmentary evidence, and year-by-year precision is largely impossible.

graph_edges.note: Continuation of the Akkadian collapse process
